tate of CaliforIlia 
Fair Political Practices CommissioIl. 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Nathan A. Simon, Mayor 
City of Fontana 
P.O. Box 518 
Fontana, CA 
92335 
Dear Mayor Simon: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/Legal 
322·5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
February 14, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Written 
Advice, Our File No. A-83-024 
I am in receipt of your recent letter requesting our advice 
as to a possible conflict of interest. 
Before we can respond to 
your request, we will need further information. 
However, I must 
first point out that we do not render advice relative to past 
conduct. 
Past conduct, if in violation of the Political Reform 
Act,* may be the sUbject of an enforcement proceeding. 
We do 
provide advice (pursuant to Section 83114(b») for pUblic 
officials as to prospective conduct. 
It is unclear from your 
letter whether you seek advice as to past conduct or prospective 
actions. 
If you desire advice as to prospective actions, please 
inform us as to what those actions will involve. 
Before we can 
give any advice we will need the following additional 
information. 
1. 
Your letter indicates that your son-in-law's only 
relationship to the card club initiative is as a contributor. 
The newspaper editorial which you enclosed (The Herald News, 
December 1, 1982) indicates that your son-in-law "has been one 
of the investors behind the scheme." 
(Emphasis added.) 
It is 
unclear what is meant by the term "investor" in the article. 
Does your son-in-law have an investment in a potential card room 
(should the measure be successful) or does he intend to have 
such an investment? 
will the passage (or non-pasage) of the 
initiative have any other financial effect on him or on his 
business? 
*Government Code Sections 81001-91014. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise noted. 


Nathan A. Simon, Mayor 
February 14, 1983 
Page Two 
2. 
When did you sell your hardware store to your son-in-law? 
Was it sold to him or to a business entity? 
If a business 
entity, what is your son-in-law's ownership interest in the 
business entity? 
Have you received a note or deed of trust on 
the real property? 
Is the purchaser making payments to you, 
either on the business or the property? 
If so, do these payments 
exceed $250 in a twelve month period? 
Specifically, who is the 
source of your retirement payments and the payments on any note 
on the real property? 
In your letter you expressed your understanding of conflicts 
of interest law as only covering a situation in which you would 
personally receive a financial benefit. 
Your understanding of 
the law regarding conflicts of interest is somewhat inaccurate. 
You do not have to personally gain or lose from an action in 
order for a conflict of interest to exist. 
It is enough if a 
source of income to you will be financially affected. 
(See, witt 
v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817.) 
You may not make~ar----­
ticipate in making, or use your official position to influence 
the making of any governmental decision which will have a 
material financial effect on a -source of income to you. 
I 
enclose a copy of our pamphlet explaining the conflict of 
interest law for your review. 
REL: km 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
j:Lr f/3:~C:, 1-
Rober t E. Leldigh I 
Counsel 
Legal Division 




NATHAN-A. SIMON 
MAYOR 
CHARLES A. KOEHLER 
MAYOR PRO TEM 
BILL FREEMAN 
DONALD F. DAY 
WILLIAM KRAGNESS 
COUNCIL MEMBERS 
------... -----_. 
JACK D. RATELLE 
CITY MANAGER 
PATRICIA M. MURRA' 
CITY CLERK 
City of Fontana 
CALIFORNIA 
January 31, 1983 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Legal Division 
P. O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Attention: 
Ms Barbara Milman, Director 
In accordance with Government Code Section 83114,(b), I hereby re-
quest a written interpretation as to whether or not a Conflict of 
Interests exists by me due to the fact that I am Mayor of the City 
of Fontana in connection with the proposed Special Municipal Elec-
tion to be held in our city March 15, 1983 due to the fact that: 
1. 
My son-in-law contributed to a fund to support the initiative 
measure to allow the voters of our City to vote as to whether 
or not we should have a card club in our City. 
JOHN D. PIAZZA 
CITY TREASURER 
2. 
I am 1/3 owner of a building that is rented by a tenant who also 
contributed to the funding for the initiative measure expenses. 
3. 
I sold my business of hardware store to my partner, who also 
is my son-in-law, and now receive a retirement payment from my 
former association in this business -
and my son-in-law contri-
buted to the card club initiative measure funding. 
It is my understanding that to violate the law re Conflict of Interest 
I must take some action to gain financially from some decision I have 
made as Mayor. 
Please let me know, at your earliest convenience, if 
I have made such a violation. 
A copy of the 
a violation. 
28th, re this 
Herald News Editorial is enclosed that indicates such 
I talked to Bob Leidigh of your office on Friday, January 
matter. 
You may wish to contact him re same. 
Thank you for your assistance. 
91ncerely, 
Enclosure 
8353 SIERRA AVENUE (P, O. Box 518) 
FONTANA. CALIFORNIA 92335 
(714) 823-3411 
SISTER CITY -
KAMLOOPS. B. C. CANADA 


A conflict 
of interest' 
Last 
week's 
announcement 
that 
the 
mysterious group of investors which has been 
working so fervently to get legalized cardroom 
gambling in Fontana includes, among others, 
our mayor's son:in-law was, to put it mildly, 
astonishing. 
Until that announcement no one seemed to 
know who was behind it all. The other four City 
Council members who serve with the mayor, at a : 
meeting last month, attacked the shroud of' 
secrecy which has surrounded the identities of 
the so-called "local citizens" whom the mayor 
claimed were urging him to support the 
proposal. The council members instructed the 
city manager to find out their names, but the 
effort proved futile. No one was talking. 
Meanwhile, Mayor Nat Simon has consistently 
claimed that he was "merely responding to the 
will of the people," tha t he had gotten involved 
only to "help some local citizens" who wanted to 
put the ma tter to a vote of the people. 
From the very beginning, of course,l the 
mayor's involvement in this entire charade has 
led to one embarassment for the city after 
another. It was he who first brought the matter 
before the council, only to find his attempt to sell 
his contemporaries fell short when they sum-
marily voted it down by a 4-1 margin. 
Then, not being able to get by the council, 
Simon apparently decided on a different tack. A 
petition began circulating to have cardroom 
gambling qualified for a special election. Simon 
claimed it was these same "local citizens" who 
were responsible for the petition, but when one of 
the original signers, a local auto dealer, was 
asked why he signed it, he said it was because I 
the mayor had asked him to. 
In another incident, it was learned that Simon! 
had appeared along with one of the proposal's 
backers before a group of senior citizens to urge 
them to help circulate the petitions. This, Simon 
claimed, would be a good way for them to raise 
funds as they would be paid for each signature 
obtained. 
Next there was the "free rent" fiasco. It was 
learned that a local shopping center owner had 
.given the petition organizers free office space in 
which to conduct their drive to get signatures. A 
shopping center owner who, by the strangest of 
coincidences. just happened to also be partners 
with the city in another shopping center project 
_ one which was highly controversial and in 
which that owner desperately needed the city 
continued support. 
When asked by a 
organizers free rent, 
had asked him to. 
he had given the 
that the mayor 
And 'now we have the coup de grace with the 
news that Mayor Simon's son-in-law, Larry 
Moniak. has been one of the investors behind the 
scheme. 
Mr. l\1oniak. of course, is a private citizen who 
may invest in whatever he pleases and need 
answer to no one. 
Mayor Simon's involvement ...: or over-
involvement to be more precise -
is another 
matter. As OUr city's highest elected official he 
has certain duties and responsibilities to those 
who elected him. Perhaps the most fundamental 
of these is tha t he conduct himself and the affa irs 
of the office of mayor with honesty and integrity. 
It now becomes painfully obvious in light of 
thIS la test revela tion tha t he has not done so. 
How long Mr.J\10niak has been involved as an 
investor is uncertain. But simple honesty and 
respect for those who entrusted him with the 
city's highest office would dictate that, at the 
ti.me his son-in-law became an investor, Mayor 
SImon should have immediately declared that he 
had a conflict of interest in the matter and ab-
stained from either voting on or discussing it in 
council meetings. 
Instead, he chose to conceal the fact while 
using the power and influence of his office to 
assist those trying to gain approval of the 
matter. 
This is, in our estimation. about as blatant an 
abuse of an elected official's authority as one is 
likely to encounter. 
• 
In making the announcement of the names of 
those backing the cardroom proposal last week, 
Mr. l\lomak commented that '"the witch hunt is 
over." 
. 
. Indeed it is. The next obvious question is what 
In fact. have we found? 
' 
AN INDEPENDENT NEWSPAPER 
eSTABLISHED JUNE 7.1923 
George E RiqqS 
M','hael E. Nut! 
4 -
The Herald·News. Fonfana, CA 
Pub1isher 
Man Ed.-
Wed .. Dec. 1.1982

sections.question: Your Request for Written Advice, Our I am in receipt of your recent letter requesting our advice as to a possible conflict of interest. Before we can respond to your request, we will need further information. However, I must first point out that we do not render advice relative to past conduct. Past conduct, if in violation of the Political Reform Act, may be the subject of an enforcement proceeding. We do provide advice (pursuant to Section 83114(b)) for public officials as to prospective conduct. It is unclear from your letter whether you seek advice as to past conduct or prospective actions. If you desire advice as to prospective actions, please inform us as to what those actions will involve. Before we can give any advice we will need the following additional information.
sections.conclusion: In your letter you expressed your understanding of conflicts of interest law as only covering a situation in which you would personally receive a financial benefit. Your understanding of the law regarding conflicts of interest is somewhat inaccurate. You do not have to personally gain or lose from an action in order for a conflict of interest to exist. It is enough if a source of income to you will be financially affected. (See, Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817.) You may not make, participate in making, or use your official position to influence the making of any governmental decision which will have a material financial effect on a source of income to you.
sections.question_synthetic: Does Mayor Simon have a conflict of interest regarding a proposed cardroom gambling initiative in Fontana given that his son-in-law is a contributor/investor in the initiative, he is a 1/3 owner of a building rented to another initiative contributor, and he receives retirement payments from his former hardware store business sold to his son-in-law?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that a conflict of interest can exist even if the official does not personally gain, but rather if a source of income to the official would be financially affected by a governmental decision. A conflict occurs when an official cannot participate in decisions that have material financial effects on their income sources. The FPPC declined to provide full advice pending clarification on whether the Mayor is seeking advice on past or prospective conduct and additional factual information about the son-in-law's investment interests and the nature of payments from the hardware store sale.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request letter from Mayor Simon (dated January 31, 1983) and the FPPC response letter from Robert E. Leidigh (dated February 14, 1983). Also includes a newspaper editorial (The Herald News, December 1, 1982) that provided context for the dispute. The FPPC response is incomplete/preliminary pending additional information from the requestor.

QUESTION: Your Request for Written Advice, Our I am in receipt of your recent letter requesting our advice as to a possible conflict of interest. Before we can respond to your request, we will need further information. However, I must first point out that we do not render advice relative to past conduct. Past conduct, if in violation of the Political Reform Act, may be the subject of an enforcement proceeding. We do provide advice (pursuant to Section 83114(b)) for public officials as to prospective conduct. It is unclear from your letter whether you seek advice as to past conduct or prospective actions. If you desire advice as to prospective actions, please inform us as to what those actions will involve. Before we can give any advice we will need the following additional information.

CONCLUSION: In your letter you expressed your understanding of conflicts of interest law as only covering a situation in which you would personally receive a financial benefit. Your understanding of the law regarding conflicts of interest is somewhat inaccurate. You do not have to personally gain or lose from an action in order for a conflict of interest to exist. It is enough if a source of income to you will be financially affected. (See, Witt v. Morrow (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817.) You may not make, participate in making, or use your official position to influence the making of any governmental decision which will have a material financial effect on a source of income to you.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to Mayor Simon's request for written advice regarding potential conflicts of interest related to a cardroom gambling initiative, noting that he misunderstands conflict of interest law (which protects sources of income, not just personal gain) and requesting additional information before providing formal advice.
